passages.explanation: When Jesus finishes all these words, he tells his disciples: you know that after two days the Passover is coming, and the Son of Man will be delivered up to be crucified. At the same time, the chief priests and elders gather in the court of the high priest Caiaphas. They conspire to seize Jesus by deceit and kill him — but not during the feast, they say, lest a riot break out. Jesus announces his death calmly; the religious leaders plot it frantically. He's more in control of the timeline than they are.

While Jesus is in Bethany at the house of Simon the leper, a woman comes with an alabaster jar of very expensive ointment and pours it on his head as he reclines at the table. The disciples are indignant: what is this waste? This could have been sold for much and given to the poor! Jesus says: why do you trouble the woman? She's done a beautiful thing. You'll always have the poor with you, but you won't always have me. In pouring this ointment on my body, she prepared me for burial. Wherever this Good News is preached in the whole world, what this woman has done will be spoken of as a memorial to her. The disciples see waste. Jesus sees worship. She understands what's coming better than the twelve do.

Then one of the twelve — Judas Iscariot — goes to the chief priests and asks: what are you willing to give me if I deliver him to you? They weigh out thirty pieces of silver for him. From that time he seeks an opportunity to betray Jesus. Thirty pieces of silver — the price of a slave in Exodus 21:32. Judas puts a price tag on the priceless.

On the first day of unleavened bread, the disciples come to Jesus: where do you want us to prepare for you to eat the Passover? He sends them to a certain man in the city with the message: the Teacher says, my time is at hand. I will keep the Passover at your house with my disciples. They prepare the Passover. When evening comes, he sits down with the twelve. As they eat, he says: most certainly I tell you, one of you will betray me. They are exceedingly sorrowful and each begins to ask: surely it isn't me, Lord? He answers: he who dips his hand with me in the dish will betray me. The Son of Man goes as it is written of him, but woe to that man through whom the Son of Man is betrayed! It would be better for him if he had not been born. Judas asks: surely it isn't me, Rabbi? Jesus says: you said it. Every disciple asks the same question — but only Judas calls Jesus Rabbi instead of Lord.

As they eat, Jesus takes bread, blesses it, breaks it, and gives it to the disciples: take, eat — this is my body. He takes the cup, gives thanks, and gives it to them: all of you drink it — this is my blood of the new covenant, which is poured out for many for the remission of sins. I tell you I won't drink of this fruit of the vine from now on until that day when I drink it anew with you in my Father's Kingdom. After singing a hymn, they go out to the Mount of Olives. Jesus reinterprets the Passover meal around himself. The bread isn't just unleavened bread — it's his body. The cup isn't just wine — it's his blood. The old covenant's meal now inaugurates the new.

Jesus tells them: all of you will be made to stumble because of me tonight. For it is written: I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered. But after I am raised up, I will go before you into Galilee. Peter answers: even if all are made to stumble, I will never be made to stumble. Jesus tells him: most certainly I tell you, tonight, before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times. Peter insists: even if I must die with you, I will not deny you. All the disciples say the same. Peter's confidence is sincere — and completely wrong. Self-knowledge isn't the same as self-assessment.

Jesus comes with them to a place called Gethsemane and tells the disciples: sit here while I go there and pray. He takes Peter and the two sons of Zebedee. He becomes sorrowful and deeply troubled: my soul is exceedingly sorrowful, even to death. Stay here and watch with me. He goes a little further, falls on his face, and prays: my Father, if it's possible, let this cup pass from me. Nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will. He comes back and finds them sleeping. He says to Peter: couldn't you watch with me for one hour? Watch and pray so you don't enter into temptation. The spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak. He goes away a second time: my Father, if this cup can't pass away unless I drink it, your will be done. He comes back and finds them sleeping again. He prays a third time with the same words. Then he comes to the disciples: sleep on now and take your rest. The hour is at hand — the Son of Man is betrayed into the hands of sinners. Get up, let's be going. He who betrays me is at hand. This is the most human Jesus has ever appeared — agonised, asking for another way, and utterly alone. The prayer isn't answered with removal of the cup but with the strength to drink it.

While he's still speaking, Judas arrives with a great multitude carrying swords and clubs, from the chief priests and elders. He gives them a sign: whomever I kiss, that's him. He immediately comes to Jesus: greetings, Rabbi! He kisses him. Jesus says: friend, why are you here? They lay hands on Jesus and seize him. One of those with Jesus draws a sword and cuts off the ear of the high priest's servant. Jesus says: put your sword back. All who take the sword will perish by the sword. Don't you think I could ask my Father and he would send me more than twelve legions of angels? But how then would the Scriptures be fulfilled? He tells the crowds: have you come out against a robber with swords and clubs? I sat daily teaching in the temple and you didn't arrest me. But all this has happened so the Scriptures of the prophets might be fulfilled. Then all the disciples leave him and flee. Jesus calls Judas 'friend.' He refuses the sword. He reminds them of their cowardice — they didn't come for him in public. And the disciples who swore they'd die with him run.

Those who seize Jesus lead him to Caiaphas the high priest, where the scribes and elders are assembled. Peter follows at a distance to the high priest's courtyard and sits with the officers to see the end. The chief priests and whole council seek false testimony against Jesus to put him to death — but find none, though many false witnesses come. Finally two come forward: this man said he could destroy the temple of God and build it in three days. The high priest asks: have you no answer? Jesus keeps silent. The high priest says: I adjure you by the living God — tell us whether you are the Christ, the Son of God. Jesus answers: you have said it. Nevertheless I tell you, from now on you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds of the sky. The high priest tears his clothes: he has spoken blasphemy! What further need do we have of witnesses? They answer: he is worthy of death. They spit in his face, beat him with fists, and slap him: prophesy to us, Christ — who hit you? Jesus' silence is strategic — he won't dignify false accusations. When pressed under oath, he answers with the most explosive claim possible: Daniel 7's Son of Man. That's not what they expected from a man in chains.

Peter sits in the courtyard. A servant girl comes: you were also with Jesus the Galilean. He denies it before everyone: I don't know what you're talking about. He goes out to the porch. Another girl sees him: this man was with Jesus of Nazareth. He denies it with an oath: I don't know the man. After a while, those standing by come to Peter: surely you're one of them — your accent gives you away. He begins to curse and swear: I don't know the man! Immediately a rooster crows. Peter remembers Jesus' words: before the rooster crows, you'll deny me three times. He goes out and weeps bitterly. Each denial escalates — from evasion to oath to cursing. And it's a servant girl, not a soldier, who breaks him. Peter's failure is devastating precisely because his love was real.
